definition: *Hydromancy* is divination by water — reading omens or visions in a bowl, spring, or pool. Its receptive, reflective medium sets it apart from the earth-figures of *geomancy* or the fire of pyromancy, and its use of a still surface for seeing images makes it the direct ancestor of scrying.
history: The word pairs Greek *hydōr* ('water') with *manteia* ('divination'), and it recurs through medieval taxonomies of the forbidden arts, usually named in one breath with geomancy, chiromancy, and pyromancy. Water's moving, reflective, receptive qualities made it a potent interpretive medium; MedievalMagicDB notes that the art survives more as a category in condemnatory lists than in dedicated manuals. Its practical heart was seeing visions in water, and, as Harkness records of the Elizabethan scryers, this fed directly into crystal-gazing: the earliest methods of scrying concentrated upon aspects of hydromancy, the seeing of visions in water.

def_sources.label: Harkness, John Dee's Conversations with Angels
def_sources.abstract: Explores why the Elizabethan scholar John Dee spent his later years trying to talk to angels through a psychic medium, arguing these sessions grew naturally out of his earlier scientific and religious interests rather than being a sign he had lost his mind.

commentary: Hydromancy is a lesson in distrusting neat taxonomies. Medieval and Renaissance writers reflexively listed it in an elemental quartet — geomancy, hydromancy, pyromancy, aeromancy — and scholars long took that scheme as descriptive. Stephen Skinner's revisionist reconstruction argues the symmetry is an artifact: 'it was only scholars who, copying Isidore of Seville, in his quest for a neat fourfold symmetrical classification, decided that hydromancy must have formed one of the four elemental forms of divination,' when in practice only geomancy really fitted. The MedievalMagicDB compilers make the complementary point — hydromancy survives as a category in condemnatory *lists* far more than as a documented practice. The field now reads such enumerations as classificatory imagination, not inventory.

commentary_sources.label: Stephen Skinner, Magical Techniques and Implements
commentary_sources.abstract: Skinner's doctoral thesis traces specific magic tools and step-by-step methods (like ritual instruments and spirit-summoning procedures) as they passed from ancient Greek-Egyptian magic texts, through Byzantine Greek manuscripts, into later European handbooks of magic — showing one continuous tradition rather than separate inventions.

etymology.note: Reading the future in water — the bowl of water behind the very idea of scrying.
etymology.hook: The oracle in a bowl of water.

flavor_snippets.html:
"I don't need your mage-rings or your mana equations. I call, and the sea answers."
—Keyta, rebel <mark>hydromancer</mark>
"Today is <mark>hydromancy</mark>? I thought it was amplimancy! I studied for amplimancy!"
